---
title: "Mathematically Investing"
description: "A logical approach to portfolio selection"
pubDate: 'May 19 2025'
tags: ['lecture', 'finance']
priority: -1
---

There is a ridiculous amount of misinformation about the stock market on the internet. This isn't suprising, as when everyone finds patterns in the random noise, and look at huge returns in cryptocurrencies and derivatives, it's very easy to draw false conclusions and pull from a variety of fallacies to justify making poor financial decisions. How do you actually decide what to invest in though? What is rational and what's a scam? Who can you trust? Why do the line go up? I'd like to offer some of my insights and explain a logical, rigorous, step-by-step approach to investing. 

**Disclaimer:** I am not a financial professional. I am not affiliated with any of the financial products discussed in this article. This is the best financial advice for the majority of people in my opinion. I hope that by reading this you will come to your own conclusions given the evidence and reasoning I present.

## TL;DR

Let's start at the end. **I invest as diversely as possible, for as cheaply as possible, whenever I can, and don't touch it**. In practice, this means investing in low-cost globally diversified index funds, in a self-directed account. I typically recommend Vanguard's Asset Allocation ETFs (tickers VBAL, VGRO, VEQT) for Canadians, though there may be more efficient alternatives if you have a large net worth.

## Part 1 - Why do the line go up?

A stock is a legal claim to a part of a publically traded company. It offers you two pieces of value:
1. The right to vote on company decisions (sometimes)
2. The right to a fraction of the companies value in the event of a liquidation or merger

The entire value of every stock is derived from these two sources. Stocks are NOT
- A right to receive dividends
- A portion of physical assets owned by the company
- A gambling machine

Though companies often pay dividends to shareholders, they are not required to, and paying a dividend does not affect the value of a stock (we'll show this later). Also, while the stock market is often tauted as being a gambling parlour (and it can certainly be used as one), this is also not where any real returns come from.

One of the most confusing parts of the stock market for new investors is understanding that a company's current value (and thus the stocks price) is not solely dependent on its current assets and revenue, but also its *expected future value*. To provide an example, lets say we have two companies: A and B; both of which are worth $100.

<!-- Company A and B -->
<!-- <div class="embed">
<img src="data:image/png;base64,"></img>
</div> -->

-|Company A|Company B
-|-|-
This year|$100|$100
Next year|$104|$200

Company A is expected to grow to $104 next year, so if you were to buy it you would be able to make a small profit. However, if you were to buy Company B now, you could double your money in a year!

Now you have $100 that you're looking to invest, which do you choose? Almost certainly Company B right? This works great **IF** you are the only person who thinks that Company B is going to double in value next year. Chances are you're trading with publically accessible information though, so as soon as the press release that Company B is going to grow comes out, you blink and the company's present valuation changes.

-|Company A|Company B
-|-|-
This year|$100|$192.30
Next year|$104|$200

And so, because everyone else would now also rather choose Company B, they bid up the price and we loose out on most of the growth. We can still buy it and make a $7.70 profit. The reason it didn't go all the way to $200 is because at this point, we'll have a 4% return on our investment either way, so everyone is now indifferent between buying Company A and Company B. 

This is just an anecdote, I could be completely lying about these numbers, but thinking critically in this way about supply and demand for stocks leads us to our first premises:

> **Premise 1.1 -** If an investment opportunity offers a strictly higher expected return as another, investors will choose to buy it instead.

> **Premise 1.2 -** If it is public knowledge that an investment opportunity offers a better return at the same cost, then its price will be bid up until it is identical to all others.

One caveat here is that while the stocks fundamentally derive value from the value of their company, their price is dictated by the expected future price of the *stock*. This is why we observe seemingly nonsensical valuations and absurd bubbles sometimes, because even if it is widespread knowledge that a stock is overvalued relative to its company, this value can only be exploited when the company is liquidated.
